coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive SERS platform based on small-sized Ag/GQDs nanozyme for intracellular analysis

Zhu, Shoujun

coredata.dc:description: The small-sized plasmonic nanoparticles are easily internalized by cells and organelles, but usually possess poor surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity, restricting their promising applications for intracellular SERS analysis. To break the current bottleneck of intracellular SERS application, herein, a uniform and small-sized (ca. 10 nm) core–shell Ag/oxidized graphene quantum dots (o-GQDs) nanohybrids have been prepared. The Ag/o-GQDs inherit unique features with parallel-interlaced stacking of o-GQDs as capping agent, possess the increasing and broadening plasmon adsorption and display a unique edge state charge transfer (CT) resonance in the long-wavelength region of visible light, enabling the enhanced electromagnetic (EM) and CT effect for superior SERS sensitivity at lower energy. Further, the Ag/o-GQDs demonstrate excellent biocompatibility and highly efficient nanozymatically catalytic activity, which are first put forward to integrate efficient SERS with Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme to catalyze peroxidase-like reaction for accurate sensing of the intracellular H2O2 at the subcellular level. Moreover, the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-catalyzed reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and the cancer cell multisubcellular-targeting capability provide a promising strategy for the synergistically catalytic therapy specifically toward tumors. This study illustrates a significant route to design an efficient small-sized noble metal/carbon nanozymatic SERS substrate, and demonstrates an exciting potential for ultrasensitive SERS bioanalysis at the subcellular level.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/reduced-GQDs (r-GQDs) nanohybrids.
UV–vis and SERS in situ monitoring of POD-like catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs in the presence of H2O2.
Detection of exogenous and endogenous H2O2 in living cells.
Optical characterization and SERS enhancement of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids.
Biocompatibility and subcellular-targeting of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids.
SERS-active Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme for in-situ monitoring of exogenous and endogenous production of H2O2–triggered POD-like catalytic reaction for intracellular sensing, imaging and catalytic therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a, b Schematic illustration for the synthesis of o-GQDs, r-GQDs, core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs, respectively. c, d HRTEM image of o-GQDs and r-GQDs, inset: HRTEM image of o-GQDs and r-GQDs with lattice spacings. e, f HRTEM images of core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs, inset: HRTEM image of Ag NPs with fringe spacings. g Dh distributions of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and individual Ag NPs measured by DLS, h Zeta potentials of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and individual Ag NPs. i, j HRTEM of single core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs: Ag core and GQDs shell with lattice spacing. k Descriptive model showing the Ag/o-GQDs structure. l XRD patterns of raw graphite, o-GQDs, r-GQDs, Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs, respectively, and the XRD standard diffraction card of Ag (bright green) and C (blue) at the bottom.

a Maximum PL excitation, and Raman spectra of Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs solution under 633 nm laser excitation wavelength; Insert: mechanism of GQDs PL quenching in Ag/GQDs nanohybrids. b UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs aqueous; Inset: photographs of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs aqueous from left to right). c The scheme of CT processes of the GQDs and Ag in core–shell Ag/GQDs. SERS spectra of PATP with different concentrations absorbed on d Ag/o-GQDs, e Ag/r-GQDs and f Ag NPs suspension under 514 nm laser excitation; Inset: SERS intensities of the characteristic peaks at 1392, 1437, and 1576 cm−1 with different concentrations of PATP, where the error bars indicate three independent measurements. 3D-FDTD simulation of the distribution of optical electric field in g Ag/o-GQDs, h Ag/r-GQDs and i Ag NPs under 514 nm laser excitation. j Schematic illustration of the SERS enhancement mechanism of the core–shell Ag/GQDs nanohybrids.

SERS spectra of PATP with different concentrations in a Ag/o-GQDs, b Ag/r-GQDs and c Ag NPs suspension under 633 nm laser excitation. Refractive index (n) and extinction coefficient (k) of d o-GQDs, e r-GQDs, f Ag. 3D-FDTD simulation of the distribution of optical electric field in g Ag/o-GQDs, h Ag/r-GQDs and i Ag NPs under 633 nm laser excitation. j The SERS stability of the Ag/o-GQDs NPs over a long period.

a-d The fluorescence, DF and fluorescence/DF overlaid images of MCF-7 cells incubated with different concentrations of Ag/o-GQDs. The cell nucleus is specially stained with Hoechst 33,342 that shows blue fluorescence and Ag/o-GQDs exhibit yellow bright scattering under the dark-field. The bio-TEM images of the MCF-7 cells incubated with e, f Ag/o-GQDs and g Ag NPs for 24 h, respectively. The NPs internalized in different organelles are labeled using the red, yellow and blue arrow for the nucleus (N), nucleoli and mitochondria (M), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a Time-dependent SERS spectra for the oxTMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs in present of H2O2 (3 × 10-3 M). The time between consecutive curves is 2 min. b Concentration-dependent SERS spectra for the oxTMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs with the H2O2 concentration range (3 × 10-3 − 3 × 10-8 M) and the blank sample. Inset: Quantitative analysis plot of oxTMB at 1605 cm−1 with different H2O2 concentrations, where the error bars indicates three independent measurements. c Schematic illustration of the SERS-active Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-triggered in situ catalytic oxTMB for cancer cell H2O2 detention and ROS-mediated therapy. d SERS spectra for the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs in MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of exogenous introduction of H2O2 (1–1000 μM). e Plots of the SERS response of oxTMB at 1605 cm−1 catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs in MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of H2O2. f SERS spectra for the oxTMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs in MCF-7 cells treated with the different concentration of PMA (0–80 μg/mL). g Plots of the SERS response of oxTMB at 1605 cm−1 catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs with H2O2 release from MCF-7 cells upon the stimulation of PMA.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive SERS platform based on small-sized Ag/GQDs nanozyme for intracellular analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The small-sized plasmonic nanoparticles are easily internalized by cells and organelles, but usually possess poor surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity, restricting their promising applications for intracellular SERS analysis. To break the current bottleneck of intracellular SERS application, herein, a uniform and small-sized (ca. 10 nm) core–shell Ag/oxidized graphene quantum dots (o-GQDs) nanohybrids have been prepared. The Ag/o-GQDs inherit unique features with parallel-interlaced stacking of o-GQDs as capping agent, possess the increasing and broadening plasmon adsorption and display a unique edge state charge transfer (CT) resonance in the long-wavelength region of visible light, enabling the enhanced electromagnetic (EM) and CT effect for superior SERS sensitivity at lower energy. Further, the Ag/o-GQDs demonstrate excellent biocompatibility and highly efficient nanozymatically catalytic activity, which are first put forward to integrate efficient SERS with Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme to catalyze peroxidase-like reaction for accurate sensing of the intracellular H2O2 at the subcellular level. Moreover, the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-catalyzed reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and the cancer cell multisubcellular-targeting capability provide a promising strategy for the synergistically catalytic therapy specifically toward tumors. This study illustrates a significant route to design an efficient small-sized noble metal/carbon nanozymatic SERS substrate, and demonstrates an exciting potential for ultrasensitive SERS bioanalysis at the subcellular level.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is promising for numerous exciting applications in chemistry, physics, biology, and medicine due to its unique characteristics of rapid, nondestructive, ultra-sensitive determination and fingerprint diagnostics [1-6]. Especially, the high molecular specificity and spatial resolution successfully advance remarkable achievements in SERS-based bioanalytical and single-cell analysis fields [7-11]. SERS technique usually focuses on plasmonic metal nanostructures (Au or Ag) with sizes over 30 nm exhibiting excellent SERS sensitivity. However, for biological applications, the plasmonic nanostructures with large size (30 nm) enter cells via endocytosis and trap in vesicles in the cytoplasm. In comparison, small-sized (e.g. lower than 10 nm) nanostructures can easily enter into many principal subcellular organelles (e.g. nucleus, mitochondria and lysosome), which are promising candidates for intracellular analysis[12,13]. Unfortunately, the small-sized plasmonic nanostructures usually present poor SERS activity (Table S1) resulting from the reduced lifetime of localized surface plasma resonance (LSPR) [14-16] and high toxicity resulting from the biological damage, significantly restricting their broad applications in biological field [17]. Therefore, developing small-sized plasmonic SERS substrates with excellent SERS sensitivity and reproducibility as well as favorable biocompatibility will potentially contribute to a great breakthrough in sensitive intracellular SERS analysis.

Recently, core–shell plasmonic nanostructures are becoming a hot research topic in the SERS field. It is notable that “shell-isolated nanoparticle-enhanced Raman spectroscopy” (SHINERS), with the plasmonic nanoparticles (NPs) as core and the inert or semiconductor materials as shell, has become a prospective and powerful tool in various chemical and biological analysis [2,18]. Especially, the graphene-isolated metal NPs (GIMNs) with better biocompatibility as one of the most promising SHINERS have been constructed for biomedical research including biosensing, imaging and therapy [19,20]. In GIMNs, the superior SERS enhancement is attributed from the synergistic effect of the electromagnetic (EM) enhancement of metal core and the charge transfer (CT) of graphene-enhanced Raman scattering (GERS). However, GIMNs have limited flexibility in the band gap regulation, limiting the diversity of CT process and CT improvement, and hampering the SERS enhancement [21]. Further, the implicate chemical-vapor-deposition (CVD) synthetic strategy for the GIMNs brings a poor hydrophilicity, restricting their application in a solution phase. Nowadays, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from graphene sheets with sizes below 10 nm have attracted enormous attention due to the exceptional properties of high optical absorptivity and hydrophilicity, excellent chemical stability, favorable biocompatibility and efficient CT property [22-25]. Moreover, the dense GQDs have also been demonstrated to possess remarkable SERS sensitivity [26]. Thus, the designable GQDs can be an ideal candidate as coating shell to prepare more compact GIMNs. Furthermore, the unique properties of quantum confinement and edge effects of GQDs can promote a series of intrinsic independent energy levels, enabling a series of CT processes (metal-GQDs CT transition) in metal/GQDs nanohybrids. Hence, the diversity of GQDs is beneficial to regulate and increase the CT of the metal/GQDs through size/edge effects, further contributing to improve the SERS enhancement, which may overcome the poor SERS sensitivity of small-sized plasmonic nanostructures.

The intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) level in tumor cells is closely related to the physiological cell response and efficacy of therapy, H2O2 is one of the major ROSs, an accurate and selective method to measure H2O2 is significant for elucidating the biological roles and underlying molecular mechanisms at the cellular level [27,28]. Recently, nanozyme-based colorimetry, electrochemistry, and fluorometry routes have been applied for intracellular H2O2 sensing due to their high sensitivity and superior catalytic activity (Table S2). However, the colorimetry and electrochemistry assays cannot realize precisely location measurements of living cells, particularly for the subcellular analysis, while the fluorometry assay is prone to photobleaching, resulting in a low stability [29-31]. SERS has huge promise at single molecule, single cell and subcellular level analysis, and the reported SERS analytical methods for sensing of H2O2 in living cells are mostly based on the H2O2-triggered oxidation reaction of the label molecules-functionalized plasmonic NPs, but the reaction rate and activity are relatively low compared to nanozymatically catalytic reaction [32,33]. Moreover, the limitation of the certain position of intracellular label-plasmonic NPs also makes it difficult to detect the intracellular information comprehensively. Therefore, it is a great demand to explore and integrate SERS and efficient nanozyme catalytic reaction to break through the bottleneck of the individual SERS and nanozyme methods for highly sensitive intracellular H2O2 determination.

In this study, we first propose an efficient strategy in designing monodispersed core–shell structured Ag/oxidized GQDs (o-GQDs) nanohybrid with a small size (ca. 10 nm). It demonstrates that the prepared Ag/o-GQDs inherit unique features with parallel-interlaced staking of o-GQDs capping agent, possess increasing and broadening plasmon adsorption and display a unique edge state CT resonance in the long-wavelength region of visible light (633 nm), enabling the enhanced EM effect and superior CT ability, contributing to remarkable SERS sensitivity in lower energy region, which breaks the current bottleneck of the poor SERS activity of small-sized plasmonic Ag NPs and benefits to Ag NPs-based SERS substrate for biological applications. In addition, the small-sized Ag/o-GQDs possess better biocompatibility and excellent inherent nucleus and mitochondria dual-targeting capability without targeting ligand modification, offering the Ag/o-GQDs unprecedented prospect for intracellular applications. Furthermore, the SERS-active Ag/o-GQDs exhibit efficient nanozymatically catalytic activity, thus the SERS integrated with Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme to catalyze peroxidase (POD)-like reaction for accurate sensing of the intracellular H2O2 at subcellular level is first put forward via the Ag/o-GQDs as highly sensitive nanozymatic SERS substrate for in situ monitoring the nanozyme-catalyzed reaction. In addition, the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-catalyzed ROS generation and the dual-targeting capability jointly maximize the tumor therapeutic efficacy. Hence, this study presents a significant strategy for designing of ultra-sensitive SERS platform based on small-sized Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme, opening up underpin potential applications in intracellular analysis at the subcellular level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/reduced-GQDs (r-GQDs) nanohybrids.
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for the synthesis of the core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs with a mean diameter of ca. 10 nm is as follows. A mixed aqueous solution consisting of AgNO3 (0.5 mmol/L), NaOH (0.01 mol/L), o-GQDs or r-GQDs (17 ug/mL) was kept at a temperature of 120 °C in oil bath for 30 min until a stable reddish brown and dark brown suspension of Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs were produced, respectively. Then the suspensions were centrifuged and washed with deionized water for two times to remove the excess GQDs, after that, the samples were redispersed in 6 mL of deionized water. The concentrations of optimized Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs were fixed to be about 8.2 × 10-4 mol/L through ICP measurement. Ag/GQDs nanohybrids with the size of 25 nm have also been prepared via the similar procedure by adjusting temperature and feeding mass ratio of GQDs to AgNO3 ([AgNO3] = 0.5 mmol/L, [o-GQDs] and [r-GQDs] = 15 μg/mL, temperature = 100 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and SERS in situ monitoring of POD-like catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs in the presence of H2O2.
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, a solution of POD-like nanocatalyst e.g. o-GQDs, r-GQDs, Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs or Ag NPs (final concentration of 1.1 × 10-4 M), TMB solution in DMSO solvent (final concentration of 1.4 × 10-4 M), and 30% of H2O2 (final concentration of 3 × 10-3 M) was added into NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0). The reaction progress was monitored at a regular time interval of 2 min by the UV–vis spectra and SERS spectra under continuous 633 nm laser excitation. For H2O2 detection, we examined the concentration-dependent SERS spectra of Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs (final concentration of 1.1 × 10-4 mM) catalytic oxidation of TMB (final concentration of 1.4 × 10-4 M) under continuous 633 nm laser excitation with H2O2 concentrations ranging from 3 × 10-3 to 3 × 10-8 M and the blank sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of exogenous and endogenous H2O2 in living cells.
ce:section.ce:para: Human breast cells (MCF-7) were seeded into the plate at 37 °C for 1 day, then the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing 15 μM Ag/o-GQDs. After incubation for 6 h, the plate was rinsed with PBS for three times to remove the unattached Ag/o-GQDs, and the medium was replaced with 1 mL PBS. Then the cells were treated with different concentrations of fresh H2O2, or Phorbol12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) that can induce the living cell to generate excessive H2O2 respectively, followed by the addition of 0.14 mM TMB at 37 °C for 1 h and then for SERS detections. SERS detections were performed by a confocal Raman system with a 633 nm laser excitation.

In this study, o-GQDs are fabricated via a modified “nano-cutting” method [34], then r-GQDs are obtained through the reduction of o-GQDs by NaBH4 (Fig. 1 a). The excellent electron-donating capability of GQDs from the abundant –OH and –NH2 groups enables fast reduction of Ag+ to Ag NPs via electron transfer to form core–shell structured Ag/GQDs nanohybrids with distinct GQDs shell arranging on the Ag NPs core (Fig. 1b). The morphologies and structures of as-prepared GQDs and Ag/GQDs are investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements. Fig. 1c and d depict both well uniform and mono-dispersed circular plane morphologies of the o-GQDs and r-GQDs with average sizes of ca. 4.5 nm. The insets respectively reveal the (100) crystal lattice of graphene (∼0.24 nm), indicating the perfect crystalline natures and the changelessness of graphene structure in o-GQDs and r-GQDs carbon core [26]. In the HRTEM images of the Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs (Fig. 1e and f), the high-contrast and uniform mono-dispersed spherical morphologies of Ag NPs with average diameters of ca. 10 nm and low contrast ultrathin GQDs shell are observed. The elemental mapping and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) further demonstrate the formation of core–shell structured Ag/GQDs nanohybrids (Fig. S1a-d). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potentials are measured for testing the stability of Ag/GQDs. DLS shows the distribution of hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and individual Ag NPs with similar sizes of around 13.6, 13.5 and 13.9 nm in sequence, which are approximately consistent with TEM results (Fig. 1g and Fig. S1e), further confirming the small-sized, uniform and mono-dispersed properties of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids. The Zeta potentials display −54.4, −57.9 and −30.8 mV for Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs respectively, indicating that the Ag/GQDs possess better stability in solution than individual Ag NPs (Fig. 1h). Therefore, in the core–shell structured Ag/GQDs, the GQDs serving as capping agents can effectively maintain the Ag NPs stability, and prevent Ag NPs from aggregation and oxidation.

In the Ag/GQDs systems, GQDs working as the capping agents play a significant role in morphology and structure control, and a crucial specific interpretation of the growth mechanism of co-assembly process are first put forward via an electron transfer process between the GQDs and the Ag+. Specifically, the Ag+ is attracted to the surface of GQDs via electrostatic interactions with abundant functional groups, the interfacial junction allows more electrons transfer from the GQDs to the Ag+ to form conductive Ag nucleation site and promote the Ag NPs growth gradually upon heating, meanwhile, the growth of Ag NPs will recruit more GQDs as capping agent decorating on their surface, contributing to the formation of core–shell structured Ag/GQDs nanohybrids. Thus, the size and morphology of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids are strongly depending on the mass ratio of GQDs to AgNO3. For instance, Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs nanohybrids with Ag NPs cores with diameters of ca. 25 nm and clear continuous carbon shells with thickness of ca. 2 nm are obtained with a reduced feeding mass ratio of GQDs to AgNO3 (Fig. S1f and g). This reasonable synthetic method offers a guidance for precisely establishing and controlling the size, structure and function of core–shell metal/GQDs nanohybrids. Furthermore, the detailed co-assembly behaviors of the interior structures of Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs are also determined by HRTEM. For Ag/o-GQDs, the mainly clear lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponding to (111) plane of Ag, and 0.20 nm and 0.14 nm attributed to the (200) and (220) planes of Ag are observed, further verifying the formation of face-centered-cubic (fcc) Ag core (Fig. 1i and Fig. S1h). The similar result is also observed for Ag/r-GQDs (Fig. 1j) [35]. However, it is noted that the d-spacing of the o-GQDs changes from original 0.24 nm into half d-spacing of 0.12 nm in Ag/o-GQDs. It is speculated that this difference may arise from the o-GQDs parallel-interlaced stacking each other behavior during the co-assembly process. The description model based on above hypothesis is performed, further demonstrating this behavior (Fig. 1k, Supporting Information Note S1). Moreover, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement is carried out to probe into the detail structural information of Ag/GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1l, the sharper and stronger diffraction peak corresponding to (002) plane of o-GQDs in Ag/o-GQDs also provides stronger evidence for our hypothesis (Supporting Information Note S2). Above all, these results are consistent with the HRTEM observation of the Ag/GQDs, certifying the co-assembly behavior in Ag/GQDs nanohybrids. However, the difference of crystal structure in Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs may be due to the diverse edge effect in o-GQDs and r-GQDs. The unique structure of o-GQDs is more inclined to form the parallel-interlaced stacking structure during the co-assembly process, contributing to a more stable and ordered crystal structure of Ag/o-GQDs, which is beneficial to significantly promote the CT. Furthermore, the surface configuration characterizedby XPS measurement also reveals that the surface group species of GQDs shell in Ag/GQDs are similar with the original GQDs, but the group proportion and contents are some different, the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 contributing to metallic Ag NPs are distinctly dominated (Supporting Information Fig. S2 and Note S3), which also indicates the successful synthesis of the Ag/GQDs nanohybrids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization and SERS enhancement of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids.
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of o-GQDs and r-GQDs are respectively measured by ultraviolet visible (UV–vis), photoluminescence (PL), Raman and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements. As illustrated, the blue-shift in the maximum optical absorption and PL emission, as well as the elevated PL intensity of r-GQDs compared to o-GQDs can be due to that the enrich –OH groups decrease the oxidation degree and increase electron density of the π structure, resulting in the higher surface state and band gap of r-GQDs (Fig. S3 and Note S4 in Supporting Information) [34,36]. Further, the differences of the surface groups also affect the GQDs reducing abilities, resulting in apparent difference in color and physical properties for the Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs (inset in Fig. 2 b and Note S5). In addition, the PL of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids are quenched owing to the CT from the GQDs to Ag to restrain the return of photoinduced electrons to the GQDs during the synthetic process, enabling to exhibit typical intrinsic carbon Raman peaks (D band and G band), which is beneficial for SERS properties (Fig. 2a). In the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2b), the Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs display strong LSPR absorption peaks deriving from Ag NPs at ca. 400 nm, representing red shift compared with the individual Ag NPs with the similar size due to an overall increment in the refractive index of the dielectric environment surrounding the Ag NPs upon GQDs coating. Additionally, the Ag/GQDs also display enhanced and broaden plasmon absorption extending into visible light range, which can be attributed to the construction of a series of efficient plasmon-induced interfacial CT processes (Ag-to-GQDs CT transition) in Ag/GQDs (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the emergence of shoulder peaks at lower energy region (500–700 nm) are observed, which may be originated from the edge state of Ag-GQDs CT processes, which is beneficial for the SERS application in biological field.

In this work, p-aminothiophenol (PATP) probes are used to determine the SERS activity. Fig. 2d-f show the SERS spectra of PATP molecules with different concentrations adsorbed on the surface of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs with the similar sizes (ca. 10 nm) and the same concentrations in aqueous dispersion under 514 nm excitation wavelength. The SERS peaks of the PATP molecules on these substrates are depicted in Table S3, which are similar to those of previous reports [35,37]. In the Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs, the adsorbed PATP can be distinguishable with a concentration as low as 10-8 M, and the enhancement factors (EF) are calculated to be about 2.39 × 106 and 2.09 × 106, respectively (EF calculation details in Note S6), which are significantly higher than previous reported small-sized SERS substrates (Table S1). However, individual Ag NPs can only detect PATP with a concentration down to 10-6 M and the EF is estimated to be about 270. We also evaluate these SERS activities under 633 nm excitation wavelength, also revealing a superior SERS activity of the Ag/GQDs over Ag NPs (Fig. 3 a-c). To investigate their SERS enhancement mechanism, the EM enhancement is systematically analyzed respectively by using 3D-finite difference time domain (FDTD) method based on the optical properties measured by ellipsometer and previous report [38] (Fig. 3d-f). The calculations reveal that the EM fields of Ag/o-GQDs and Ag/r-GQDs are both enhanced compared with Ag NPs under 514 and 633 nm wavelengths (Fig. 2g-i, Fig. 3g-i), demonstrating that the introduction of GQDs contributes to a certain enhancement function on the EM of Ag/GQDs, which is also consistent with the plasmon enhancement in UV–vis spectra. Moreover, the GQDs shell is similar to graphene which may have GERS properties attributed to CT, and the Ag/GQDs EM enhancement can also improve interfacial CT ability between Ag/GQDs and probe molecules. Additionally, the GQDs with a series of energy levels can provide various Ag-to-GQDs CT complex, especially the CT resonance can further improve the SERS activity. The band gap of Ag/o-GQDs is 1.96 eV, which is resonant with 633 nm, and the band gap of Ag/r-GQDs is 2.16 eV, which is resonant with 574 nm (Fig S3k, l and Note S4). Therefore, the edge state CT resonance of Ag/o-GQDs can enable the superior CT ability, then contributing to remarkable SERS sensitivity under 633 nm excitation, which will not cause serious damage to biological environment, and beneficial for Ag NPs based SERS substrate for biological applications. In this case, the hot spots and electrons of the Ag NPs created via LSPR could penetrate ultrathin GQDs to pass through successfully due to the GQDs good permeability to rival graphene, producing hot spots (internal and external nanogap) and electrons on GQDs surface in Ag/GQDs nanohybrids, then the EM and CT enhancement synergistically rendering the small-sized Ag/GQDs with remarkable SERS sensitivity, which breaks through the poor SERS sensitivity of the small-sized plasmonic NPs (Fig. 2j). Besides, the SERS intensities of PATP remain almost unchanged after Ag/o-GQDs are stored for a long time, indicating their excellent SERS long-term stability (Fig. 3j). This study presents a strong theoretical framework and offers significant direction for designing ultra-sensitive SERS platform based on the noble metal/carbon nanohybrids, opening up underpin potential applications in analysis, catalysis and biology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and subcellular-targeting of Ag/GQDs nanohybrids.
ce:section.ce:para: To explore unprecedented prospects of the Ag/GQDs in biological and intracellular applications, the cytotoxicity tests of Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs are determined (Fig. S4). As illustrated, the Ag/o-GQDs possess better biocompatibility, and the viability of the MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of Ag/o-GQDs ranging from 0 to 15 μM is higher than 100%. However, when the concentration increases to 20 μM, the cell viability is rapidly decreased by 89%. Thus, to minimize the cytotoxicity of the Ag/o-GQDs, the concentration of 15 μM of Ag/o-GQDs is chosen for further study. Fig. S5 also shows the bright-field images of the MCF-7 cells incubated with Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs with the same concentrations of 15 μM, respectively. It is observed that the morphologies of most cells incubated with Ag/o-GQDs have no change, and only few cells gradually shrink. However, the cells incubated with Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs display more obvious cell damage, further demonstrating the excellent biocompatibility of Ag/o-GQDs. Inductive Coupled Plasma Emission Spectrometer (ICP) measurement has been used to evaluate the Ag+ releasing from Ag/o-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs with the same concentrations of Ag element, revealing that 0.015%, 0.045% and 8.2% of Ag+ can release from the above samples. These results suggest that the introduction of the unique structure of o-GQDs shell in Ag/o-GQDs can effectively prevent the dissociation of Ag ions from Ag NPs and significantly reduce the cell toxicity and improve the biocompatibility, which enables a promising prospect for intracellular applications.

Therefore, owing to the small size and superior biocompatibility properties of the Ag/o-GQDs, they are expected to be applicable for targeting subcellular organelles in living cells, for example, nucleus and mitochondria are the vital organelles with responsibilities that maintain cell operation and metabolism, which have become prime therapeutic targets in different diseases and states [39,40]. Furthermore, the nucleus and mitochondria dual-targeted NPs for effective anticancer applications are gaining increasing attention by attacking double key sites of tumor cells to improve the targeting delivery efficiency significantly and enhance therapeutic efficacy [41]. Thus, it is imperative to develop dual-targeted NPs to enhance antitumor activity. The positive GQDs have also been reported and engineered for efficiently targeting the cell nucleus and mitochondria to enhance the therapeutic efficacy [42,43]. Herein, the prepared small-sized core–shell Ag/GQDs may also possess excellent dual-targeted ability. Firstly, the fluorescence, dark-field (DF) and fluorescence/dark-field (DF) imaging of MCF-7 cells incubating with Ag/o-GQDs corresponding to the concentration of the vitro cytotoxicity test are performed to identify the locations and confirm the nucleus targeting ability of the Ag/o-GQDs (Fig. 4 a-d). It can be observed that with the increment of Ag/o-GQDs concentrations, more Ag/o-GQDs are centering on the nucleus region and distributing in the cytoplasm, or even possibly some of them have been delivered to nucleolus, proving the label-free nucleus-targeting of Ag/o-GQDs. Furthermore, the Ag/o-GQDs can also fully enter into nucleus at the critical toxic concentration of 15 μM, indicating the high nucleus-targeting capacity of Ag/o-GQDs.

To get a clear insight into the targeting performance of theses NPs in cells, the bio-TEM images are employed. As shown in Fig. 4e-g, the nucleoplasm has a clear and high contrast boundary and mitochondria displaying brighter contrast domains. It can be observed that both the Ag/o-GQDs and Ag NPs can enter into the cells without any premodification and the Ag/o-GQDs are almost singly dispersed in the mitochondria and the cell nucleus, even can be located in nucleolus, while some locate in the lysosome and cytoplasm (Fig. 4e-f and Fig. S6a). However, by contrast, most of Ag NPs are accumulated in the mitochondria with an aggregation state, also some locate in the cytoplasm, and it is difficult to observe Ag NPs in the nucleus after extensive searching (Fig. 4g and Fig. S6b-d). These results demonstrate that the Ag/o-GQDs possess an intrinsic nucleus and mitochondria dual-targeting capability, providing them great potential for label-free selective detention and tumor therapeutics. We consider that the nucleus internalization of the Ag/o-GQDs is strongly influenced by their small size and surface groups. The previous reports have demonstrated that the GQDs can enter the cell nucleus, and the small-sized Ag/o-GQDs also likely penetrate the nucleus by diffusion [42-44]. Nevertheless, the surface group effect may be another important factor for Ag/o-GQDs nucleus-targeting, the abundant carbonyl groups on the surface of Ag/o-GQDs may selectively attach to amino groups of nucleic acids (DNA or RNA) in the nucleus or o-GQDs interact with nucleic acids via π–π stacking, driving the Ag/o-GQDs overcome the barrier of nuclear membrane to the nucleus. To further verify the above hypothesis and the nucleus-targeting capacity of Ag/o-GQDs, a double-stranded DNA (λ DNA) chip are prepared and examined under a cell nuclei-mimicking condition [45]. λ DNA is a pervasive molecule that is present in cell nucleus, the affinity ability of λ DNA with Ag/o-GQDs can be evaluated and predicted for the nucleus-targeting capacity of Ag/o-GQDs. From Fig. S6e and f, it is observed that Ag/o-GQDs can much more effectively adsorb on the λ DNA chips, indicating the high affinity between Ag/o-GQDs and λ DNA, and conducing to effectively nucleus-targeting. As Ag/o-GQDs and λ DNA are both negatively charged, their affinity is not due to the electrostatic interaction but the specific surface chemistry, which is consistent with the abovementioned analysis. We envisage that the better biocompatibility and dual-targeted properties of Ag/o-GQDs would provide a more effective strategy for significant improving the targeting delivery efficiency and enhancing therapeutic efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS-active Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme for in-situ monitoring of exogenous and endogenous production of H2O2–triggered POD-like catalytic reaction for intracellular sensing, imaging and catalytic therapy.
ce:section.ce:para: The unique physicochemical properties of GQDs, particularly small size, good biocompatibility and efficient CT ability endow them as promising nanozymes for biomedical applications. In particular, they have shown great promise in ROS-mediated tumor catalytic sensing and therapy [46-48]. Moreover, the Ag NPs as noble metal nanozymes also display enzyme-like catalytic ability [49]. Hence, the construction of core–shell structured Ag/GQDs nanohybrids may endow them with synergistically enhanced catalytic activity for POD mimicking. In this study, the prepared Ag/o-GQDs are also demonstrated to be an efficient nanozyme with unprecedented POD-like activity by monitoring the catalytic oxidation of a typical POD substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S7, a typical oxidized TMB (oxTMB) with a maximum absorbance at 650 nm has been observed, indicating the efficient POD-like activity of the Ag/o-GQDs. By contrast, we have compared the POD-like activity of Ag/o-GQDs with those of o-GQDs, r-GQDs, Ag/r-GQDs and Ag NPs by UV–Vis absorption spectra, it is clearly observed that the Ag/o-GQDs exhibit the highest catalytic activity. The significantly enhanced POD-like activity of the Ag/o-GQDs can be essentially attributed to the remarkable synergistic plasmon enhancement and CT improvement. As known, the GQDs possess high adsorption capacity and efficient CT ability due to the large specific area with small size and unique π-conjugation structure. In the Ag/GQDs, the plasmon enhancement can also accelerate the electrons transfer from Ag NPs to GQDs, and then increase the electron mobility and density to synergistically catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into OH· radicals to oxidize the TMB [50,51]. The synergistic effect of the Ag and GQDs jointly promote the nanozymatically catalytic efficiency. Furthermore, the oxygen functional groups of o-GQDs may also play an important role in improving CT effect. A density functional theory (DFT) study proves that the functionalization of graphene surfaces with carbonyl and carboxylic groups can significantly contribute to the reduction of H2O2 to OH· [52,53]. The o-GQDs own more carboxylic/carbonyl groups than r-GQDs, which are beneficial to the H2O2 reduction. Moreover, the distinct structure of the o-GQDs parallel-interlaced stacking each other in Ag/o-GQDs causes the o-GQDs crystal structure more ordered, which will also accelerate interfacial CT efficiency, thereby further enhancing the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme with unprecedented POD-like catalytic activity.

As aforementioned, the prepared Ag/o-GQDs possess both efficient POD-like catalytic activity and excellent SERS sensitivity, providing an efficient platform for in-situ SERS monitoring of the nanozyme-catalyzed reaction. The SERS technique can enable more accurate monitoring of the changes of adsorbed molecules and actual catalytic process on the surface of the catalyst. Fig. 5 a displays the SERS spectra of the oxTMB molecules catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme with 633 nm laser excitation, showing efficient SERS and nanozymatic activity. We believe that the efficient SERS activity originates from the EM enhancement, the new merging CT resonance at long wavelength of Ag/o-GQDs, CT resonance of oxTMB and the increase CT effect between Ag/o-GQDs and oxTMB. The SERS characteristic peaks of the oxTMB at around 1190, 1336, and 1605 cm−1 attributed to CH3 bending modes, inter-ring C−C stretching modes, and C−H bending+ring stretching modes are clearly observed, which are consistent with the previous reports [35,54]. During the process of the catalytic reaction, the SERS intensities of oxTMB peaks increase gradually with time, and the reaction equilibrium is achieved in 30 min. We have also compared the POD-like activity of the Ag/o-GQDs with that of the Ag/r-GQDs by SERS, both of which display better linear relationships, also further demonstrating that the Ag/o-GQDs possess superior catalytic efficiency over Ag/r-GQDs (Fig. S8 and S9). Since H2O2 is involved in the POD-like catalytic reaction and its concentration is strongly correlated with the SERS intensities of the oxTMB molecules, a facile H2O2 detection strategy has been developed. As shown in Fig. 5b, the SERS intensities of the characteristic peaks of oxTMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs increase with the increase of H2O2 concentrations. In addition, there is a good linear relationship based on the characteristic peak at 1605 cm−1, and the H2O2 concentration as low as 4.22 × 10-9 M can be identified. By contrast, the detection limit for H2O2 is calculated to be about 8.60 × 10-9 M for the Ag/r-GQDs (Fig. S8c). Both of the two values are lower than those nanosensors reported previously (Table S4), demonstrating the remarkable H2O2 sensing of Ag/GQDs. This study makes it possible for in situ super-sensitive SERS motoring the Ag/GQDs nanozyme-catalyzed reaction and accurate detection of H2O2, broadening their application for in situ SERS monitoring and detection of heterogeneous catalytic reactions.

In the following, we first put forward an efficient route for in situ monitoring and detecting of intracellular H2O2 by SERS integrated with the catalytic reaction due to the excellent subcellular internalization of Ag/o-GQDs and the high sensitivity for H2O2 sensing (Fig. 5c). This strategy possesses a high SERS sensitivity and excellent nanozymatically catalytic properties, which can also overcome the certain position limitation of the label-nanostructures for intracellular SERS detection and is beneficial to realize precisely locate regional measurement and comprehensive detection in intracellular space at subcellular level. In the cancer treatment, the sensing capability of Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme in detecting intracellular H2O2 can be realized from exogenous introduction or endogenous generation by PMA stimulation. For the exogenous introduction of H2O2, the SERS spectrum of oxTMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs exhibits a dramatic SERS response and the typical peaks at 1160, 1340 and 1605 cm−1 in living cells are clearly observed, which are almost similar to those in vitro. Furthermore, the SERS intensities of the oxTMB increase with the introducing exogenous H2O2 concentration, even also can be discernible of exogenous H2O2 concentration as low as 0.1 μM (Fig. 5d). Moreover, Fig. 5e displays a linear SERS response over the increasing concentration of exogenous H2O2 (0–90 μM), enabling to accurately measure the H2O2 mean concentration of 3.17 μM in cancer cells, which can well meet the biological detection [55,56]. These results indicate that the Ag/o-GQDs also possess efficient intrinsic POD-like activity in cancer cells similar to in vitro, which can be related to the catalyzed OH· generation due to the tumor microenvironment (TME) feature with unique characteristics of mild acidity, overproduced H2O2 and hypoxia. Notably, this strategy for the H2O2 detection in cancer cells also guarantees excellent reproducibility through evaluating the distributions of the in situ measured SERS intensities of the typical peak at 1605 cm−1 in 30 different cells and 10 locations at the subcellular level (Fig. S10a). Furthermore, there are no typical SERS peaks in the systems of MCF-7, MCF-7+TMB, MCF-7+PMA, MCF-7+Ag/o-GQDs, MCF-7+Ag/o-GQDs+H2O2, MCF-7+Ag/o-GQDs+PMA, which is beneficial and reliable to ensure the interference-free cancer cell SERS detection in the nanozyme-catalyzed systems (Fig. S10b). In addition, the morphologies of the cells become increasingly shrinking and turn into the sphere with the increment of the H2O2 concentration observed by Raman bio-imaging (Fig. S11). The visual cells damage indicates that the efficient Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme can promote the decomposition of exogenous H2O2 for rapid toxic OH· generation, which can be expected for highly efficient tumor catalytic therapy.

To achieve the accurate and quantitative detection of H2O2 distribution and in situ monitoring dynamic release of H2O2 in cells, as well as the evaluation of the therapeutic efficiency, PMA is employed. As shown in Fig. 5f, the typical SERS peaks of the oxTMB catalyzed by Ag/o-GQDs with endogenous H2O2 by PMA stimulation is similar to that of exogenous H2O2 in cancer cells, but the peak positions show a few shifts to 1070, 1340 and 1605 cm−1 which can be considered the slightly changes in the cancer cell environment. Furthermore, the SERS intensities of the oxTMB peaks increase upon the addition of PMA, indicating that the release rates of H2O2 are dependent on the PMA concentration. Moreover, there is a linear relationship between the SERS intensities at 1605 cm−1 and PMA at lower and higher concentrations, respectively. We calculate that PMA stimulate MCF-7 cells generating H2O2 at a rate of 5.9 × 10-13 cell/h based on the linear correlation with a concentration range of PMA (0–12 μM) and the number of cells used in the measurements (1.3 × 106 cells), which is in agreement with previously reported measurements (Fig. 5g) [33]. These results demonstrate that the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme has a high sensitivity to determine the endogenous H2O2 released in TME. Additionally, the cell viability also decreases and the cell damage is more obviously with the increasing of PMA by Raman bioimaging (Fig. S12). However, in the absence of the Ag/o-GQDs nanohybrid, these dosages of PMA cannot significantly damage the cancer cells, indicating that the endogenous H2O2 is probably consumed through multiple metabolism processes (Fig. S12a and b) [29]. Consequently, the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme can further accelerate the decomposition into toxic OH· generation from PMA stimulated endogenous H2O2, endowing the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme for highly efficient tumor catalytic therapy. In this regard, we successfully demonstrate the excellent capability of Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme in detecting intracellular H2O2 by introducing exogenous or produced endogenous H2O2, providing a highly sensitive SERS platform for tumor subcellular sensing. Furthermore, the synergistic effect of the ROS generation by Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-catalytic oxidative stress and the multi-subcellular targeting capacity jointly contribute to significantly maximize the tumor therapeutic efficacy. This study proposes a promising synergetic strategy for tumor catalytic subcellular sensing and enhanced tumor therapeutic efficacy, which will advance the development of nanozymes in cell systems.

In this study, small-sized core–shell structured Ag/o-GQDs inheriting unique features with parallel-interlaced staking of o-GQDs capping agent have been prepared. The Ag/o-GQDs possess superior SERS enhancement resulting from the unique optical properties and synergistic effect of enhanced EM and efficient CT, which breaks through the current bottleneck of poor SERS sensitivity of the small-sized plasmonic NPs. Particularly, the excellent SERS enhancement at lower energy region is beneficial for the Ag NPs-based SERS substrate in biological applications. Moreover, Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-based SERS strategy for sensing the intracellular H2O2 has been first demonstrated due to the dramatic POD-like catalytic activity of Ag/o-GQDs, providing an ideal platform for intracellular in situ SERS monitoring the nanozymatically catalytic reactions and successful sensing at the subcellular level. Additionally, the synergistic effect of the Ag/o-GQDs nanozyme-catalyzed ROS generation and realizing intrinsic nucleus and mitochondria dual-targeting property significantly improve the tumor catalytic therapy efficiency, which may open new opportunities for synergistically catalytic therapy. These observations in this study may revolutionarily change the landscape of biomedical research and bring significant advances in material science, chemistry and catalysis systems.

Wei Song, Shuping Xu, Bing Zhao conceived the idea, planned and directed the project, and revised the manuscript. Jiaqi Wang performed the cell culture and cell viability. Linjia Li performed the synthesis, characterizations. Yu Tian performed the electromagnetic field distribution simulations. Shoujun Zhu, Bai Yang revised the manuscript. Yuping Zhang performed the ellipsometry measurements.
